title: Security Drivers and the Security Driver API
breadcrumbs: Security Drivers and the Security Driver API
text: Security Drivers and the Security Driver API Uniface provides security drivers of two types: Client/Server security driver—encode and decode connect messages and message header data that are exchanged between the Uniface client and Uniface Router. Email security drivers for SMTP and POP3 authentication—negotiate authentication via a SASL (Simple Authentication and Security Layer) mechanism. These security drivers are implementations of the Uniface Security Driver API, which you can use to create your own security driver. For more information, see APIs: Security Driver . To help you use the API, the C source files for the security drivers are available as a sample on Rocket Community . The compiled libraries for the drivers provided by Uniface are located in the /common/bin (Windows) or /common/lib (Unix) directory of your Uniface installation. To make a security driver available to your application, you must specify the library and entry point in the [USER_3GL] section of the assignment file. For example: [USER_3GL] ; C/S security driver:C:/Program Files (x86)/Uniface10/bin/zsecdrv(usecappl) ; OAuth 2.0 security drivers for email:C:/Program Files (x86)/Uniface10/bin//uauthxoauth2(UAuthXOAUTH2POP, UAuthXOAUTH2SMTP) Copy Related Links APIs: Security Driver Uniface provides an API for creating custom client/server and SMTP and POP security drivers. The API include files for the C compiler and libraries are provided in the Uniface/3gl directory. SMTP and POP3 Email Uniface provides support for sending and receiving email using the SMTP and POP protocols over TCP/IP or TLS. Security Context A security context is the complete security environment required for a secure connection to a mail server. It consists of one or more security drivers that are responsible for connection and authentication to the email server. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
